core_takeaway: This is a clear counterexample to first-position advantage: Agent_1 opened with ambitions for Stone/Quill, but the process converged on a low-friction anchor where Agent_5 kept Quill and Agent_1 accepted Apple, producing Agent_1=6 versus Agent_5=72.
why_or_counterexample: Agent_1 had the first public move but did not convert it into priority-item control. The decisive constraint was Agent_5's hard Quill claim plus a consensus norm around identical anchors, so the last position received its highest-value item while the first received a low-value fallback.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Agent_1 initially pursued Stone and Quill, but Agent_5 set a firm Quill red line. In the second discussion cycle, Agent_1 and most others endorsed the anchor: Quill to Agent_5, Jewel to Agent_2, Apple to Agent_1, Pencil to Agent_3, Stone to Agent_4.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Private thinking is available. Agent_1 recognized that Quill and Stone were its strongest targets but hard to secure under unanimity, while Agent_5 explicitly saw the same anchor as maximizing its own utility.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: All five formal proposals were the same allocation. The accepted Proposal #5 did not differ substantively from Agent_1's proposal; the whole proposal phase simply formalized the public anchor.
phase_notes.private_voting: All private votes accepted all proposals. Agent_1 accepted because it got Apple worth 6, while Agent_5 accepted because the identical anchor secured its optimal Round 1 outcome.
phase_notes.reflection: No reflection entries are present in this transcript.

mechanisms.theme: Agent_5's Quill red line displaced the opener
mechanisms.explanation: The last speaker publicly made Quill non-negotiable at very high value, and Agent_1 later endorsed locking Quill with Agent_5. This gave the last position a stronger process constraint than the first speaker's opening agenda.

evidence.quote: Quill is my number-one item (valued at 72).
I advocate publicly locking Quill with Agent_5 for Round 1
It keeps Quill with its top-value holder (Agent_5)

mechanisms.theme: Agent_1 traded away its own high-value agenda for consensus
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1's initial preferences centered on Stone and Quill, but its private reasoning treated those items as too hard to win under unanimity. In voting, Agent_1 accepted the anchor because it merely received Apple worth 6.

evidence.quote: heftiest items for me but also the hardest to secure unilaterally

mechanisms.explanation: The formal proposal phase did not reward Agent_1's first proposal; every agent submitted the same allocation and every proposal was unanimously accepted. The final selection of Proposal #5 reflected identical consensus, not first-speaker leverage.

mechanisms.theme: Utility followed value asymmetry, not speaking position
mechanisms.explanation: The accepted anchor matched Agent_5's 72-point top item while leaving Agent_1 with a 6-point item. The large first-minus-last gap is explained by who secured which item, not by Agent_1's initial speaking slot.

mechanisms.theme: Unanimity pressure favored a low-friction anchor over first-mover bargaining
mechanisms.explanation: Private reasoning repeatedly prioritized unanimous acceptance and avoiding deadlock. That process pressure made agents accept a clean anchor quickly, even when it left Agent_1 far below Agent_5.

evidence.quote: without risking a zero-outcome if negotiations stall
